title: Leaning Tower of Pisa
subtitle: For questions 1–8, read the text below and decide which answer (A, B, C or D) best fits each gap. There is an example at the beginning (0).
passage:
Scientists and engineers have long been [[0]] by the Leaning Tower of Pisa's remarkable ability to remain standing over several centuries despite its almost four-degree tilt and having been at the [[1]] of numerous earthquakes since its construction was completed in 1372. To the naked eye, it appears extremely precarious and likely to collapse at any moment, and yet it has [[2]] the test of time. New research suggests that the tower's [[3]] can be attributed to the soil it is built on and a pause early on in the construction works that had the unintended effect of strengthening the foundations.

Over the course of the 12th century, the city of Pisa engaged in several battles with other nearby towns and cities which caused construction to cease. Experts now suggest that in all [[4]] this was responsible for the tower being so [[5]] since it allowed it to gradually sink further into the soft soil. [[6]], the soft soil which caused it to lean in the first place has [[7]] to be its saviour too. Restoration work carried out in the early 20th century to reinforce the foundations has [[8]] confidence amongst scientists and engineers, and they believe that the tower is unlikely to collapse any time soon. In fact, not for at least another two hundred years.

items.explainCorrect: At the mercy of is a fixed phrase: unable to prevent harm from something powerful (here, earthquakes).

explainIfChosen.1: At the forefront of means leading or most advanced — it does not collocate with being helpless against earthquakes.
explainIfChosen.2: At the expense of signals a trade-off (one thing sacrificed for another), not physical danger.
explainIfChosen.3: On the spot means immediately; it does not fit this pattern after at the.

items.explainCorrect: Stand the test of time is idiomatic: still acceptable or strong after a long period.

explainIfChosen.0: We do not say hold the test of time; the verb is stand.
explainIfChosen.1: Last the test of time is not the usual collocation — stand is fixed.
explainIfChosen.3: Remain the test of time is not idiomatic English.

items.explainCorrect: Resilience fits a structure that survives stress and keeps standing — the tower bouncing back from shocks.

explainIfChosen.1: Fortitude is inner strength of a person, not normally used for a building in this way.
explainIfChosen.2: Tenacity is persistence (often of people); awkward for the tower as subject here.
explainIfChosen.3: Resistance alone is vaguer; resilience better captures surviving and recovering.

items.explainCorrect: In all likelihood means very probably — a standard formal phrase.

explainIfChosen.0: We say in the event of, not in all eventuality in this sense.
explainIfChosen.1: In all appearances is not a natural fixed phrase here.
explainIfChosen.2: Against all odds is idiomatic; in all odds is not.

items.explainCorrect: Stable describes the tower settling safely in the ground despite the lean.

explainIfChosen.0: Concrete suggests the material, not the structural steadiness meant here.
explainIfChosen.2: Anchored is possible but less natural than stable for overall steadiness in soft soil.
explainIfChosen.3: Substantial stresses size or importance, not the safety of the lean.

items.explainCorrect: Ironically: the same soft soil caused the lean but also helped save the tower — a neat contrast.

explainIfChosen.0: Flippantly suggests joking tone; the sentence is factual, not casual mockery.
explainIfChosen.2: Incongruously stresses mismatch of things side by side; the link here is cause saving from danger.
explainIfChosen.3: Ambiguously suggests unclear meaning; the situation is paradoxical but clear.

items.explainCorrect: It transpired that… = it turned out / came to be known — fits formal reporting.

explainIfChosen.0: Reveal usually needs an object (it revealed) or a different structure.
explainIfChosen.1: Arisen to be is not natural; we do not use arise this way with to be its saviour.
explainIfChosen.2: Materialised to be is awkward; transpired fits the register.

items.explainCorrect: Instil confidence is a strong collocation: make people feel confident.

explainIfChosen.0: Transfuse is literal (blood); not used with abstract confidence.
explainIfChosen.3: Permeate suggests spreading through a substance; instil matches people and feelings.
